reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: The output table (columns, values, descriptions) is directly grounded in the source's `tuiManager.printTable` call — no invented columns, accurate role labels (Owner/Admin/Developer/Viewer) matching ROLE_LABELS.
Role descriptions and the 'Current' column semantics match the source code exactly (`organization.isCurrent ? 'yes' : 'no'`).
The Output formats section correctly describes both `--outputFormat jsonl` and `--agent` as separate invocations with one command per fenced block — no copy-button trap.
`<CliCommandsApiReference>` is used correctly with all three flags from the source (`agent`, `logLevel`, `outputFormat`) present in `sortedArgs`.
Related commands are well-chosen and cover the natural navigation cluster (create, delete, whoami, login).
No AWS jargon, no marketing language, clean prose throughout — appropriate for the stated audience.
problems: The 'Examples' section duplicates the `stacktape org:list --outputFormat jsonl` command already shown in 'Output formats'. The 'Pipe organization data into another tool' label doesn't differentiate it from the earlier section, and a first-time reader scanning from top to bottom will see the same command twice with no new information.

The edge case where the account has no organizations is not mentioned. The source (`commandOrgList`) emits a `tuiManager.warn('No organizations found for this user.')` — first-time users who just signed up may hit this and wonder if the command failed.

The first paragraph calls the concept the 'current organization for your API key', which is subtly different from how the source describes it ('current organization for your API key' in the command description, but 'current Stacktape user' in the output). The page is internally consistent, but a sentence clarifying that one API key can map to one active org at a time would help first-timers understand why there's a 'Current' column at all.
optionalImprovements: Replace the duplicate `--outputFormat jsonl` example in the 'Examples' section with a genuinely distinct example (e.g., `--logLevel debug` already present is fine) or remove the redundant entry and let 'Output formats' carry that content.

Add a one-sentence note after the command description or in a callout: 'If no organizations appear, the command prints a warning and returns an empty list — verify your API key with `stacktape info:whoami`.' This handles the no-organization edge case the source explicitly handles.

Add a one-sentence clarification of the 'Current' column: 'The active organization determines which projects and AWS accounts are visible to subsequent commands. Use `org:create` and re-login to switch.' This helps readers understand why the column matters, not just what it contains.

The 'Output formats' section is three paragraphs plus two code blocks — a fairly heavyweight H2 for content that mostly just restates that `--outputFormat` and `--agent` exist (both already in the flags reference). Consider collapsing it to a short note under 'Usage' or a single `<Info>` callout, so the flags reference isn't reached only after a full section.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Source-grounded column table: every column name (Organization, Role, Connected AWS, Current, ID) and the four role display values (Owner, Admin, Developer, Viewer) exactly match the ROLE_LABELS map in src/commands/org-list/index.ts.
Output format examples are correctly split into separate fenced blocks — no copy-buffer trap.
Flags table (agent, logLevel, outputFormat) matches commands.ts exactly; no extra or missing flags.
Lead paragraph is concise, self-contained, and accurate for AEO extraction.
Related commands are well-chosen and cover the obvious next steps (org:create, org:delete, info:whoami, login).
problems: The 'Examples' section repeats `stacktape org:list --outputFormat jsonl` which was already shown in the 'Output formats' section. This is a clear duplicate that adds no new information and weakens the page's Examples section.

The empty-organization case is silently omitted: src/commands/org-list/index.ts shows that when `organizations.length === 0`, the command prints a warning ('No organizations found for this user.') and returns early. A user who runs the command and sees this warning has no guidance in the docs.

No FAQ section. While the page's narrow scope means a full 8-10 FAQ is not warranted, 3-4 targeted Q&As (e.g. 'How do I switch the current/active organization?', 'What does the Current column indicate?', 'What roles are available?', 'The command says No organizations found — why?') would meaningfully serve readers who arrive via search.
optionalImprovements: Remove the duplicate `stacktape org:list --outputFormat jsonl` example from the Examples section. The 'Output formats' section already covers it with context; the Examples block should show genuinely different invocation patterns (the `--logLevel debug` one is fine and earns its place).

Add a one-sentence note under the column table or in a short 'Edge cases' paragraph: 'If your account has no organizations, the command prints a warning and exits without a table.' This is directly sourced from the `tuiManager.warn` branch in the command implementation.

Add a small FAQ section (3-5 Q&As) covering: what 'Current' means and how to switch active organization, what the available roles are and their permissions, and what to do when no organizations appear. These are the questions readers will have after running the command for the first time.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: The page documents exactly one CLI command and uses the required <CliCommandsApiReference command="org:list" sortedArgs={...} /> component.
The basic usage example is copyable and correctly shows that no command-specific flags are required.
The output table columns match src/commands/org-list/index.ts, including Organization, Role, Connected AWS, Current, and ID.
Shell examples follow the one-command-per-block rule and avoid explanatory comments inside bash blocks.
problems: The opening paragraph is only about 35 words, below the 40-75 word answer-first target for extractable snippets.
Some wording is slightly broader than the source: "currently active for CLI operations" should be narrowed to "current organization for your API key," which is the source-backed phrase.
The Usage section starts with a code block, so the first explanatory paragraph under the H2 is not a self-contained answer-first section paragraph.

The Output formats section claims the command renders an "interactive table" by default; the command source shows tuiManager.printTable, while output format behavior is mostly grounded in the shared outputFormat flag description, so this should be phrased more cautiously.
The page omits the source-backed empty-result behavior: when no organizations are found, the command warns "No organizations found for this user." and returns an empty list.
The page does not mention that the internal MEMBER role is displayed as Developer, which is a useful grounded detail from ROLE_LABELS.
The Related commands list has four entries, while the CLI page instruction asks for the 1-3 closest siblings.
optionalImprovements: Expand the opening paragraph to 40-75 words and use the source-backed wording: organizations accessible with the current Stacktape user/API key, role, connected AWS account count, and current organization for the API key.
Move the flags reference directly after the usage example if matching the CLI page rhythm strictly: lead paragraph, usage example, auto-generated reference, examples, related commands.
Add a short note about the empty state: if no organizations are available, Stacktape prints "No organizations found for this user."
Clarify role display labels: OWNER, ADMIN, DEVELOPER, VIEWER are shown as Owner, Admin, Developer, Viewer, and MEMBER is displayed as Developer.
Trim Related commands to the closest 1-3 commands, likely org:create, org:delete, and info:whoami.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is largely grounded in the provided CLI command definition and runtime implementation. I found one concrete issue: the output-format prose overstates the default terminal behavior compared with the generated flag description embedded in the draft and should be narrowed to auto-detection.

issues.statement: “By default, `org:list` renders an interactive table in your terminal.”
issues.reason: The command implementation prints a table, but the draft's own generated flag reference says `outputFormat` is auto-detected and that `plain` is used automatically in CI or non-TTY environments. The prose should not imply that interactive TTY output is always the default.
issues.suggestedFix: Change to: “When Stacktape detects an interactive terminal, `org:list` renders a table. In CI or other non-TTY contexts, Stacktape can use plain output automatically; use `--outputFormat jsonl` or `--agent` for machine-readable output.”

positiveFindings: The documented command invocation `stacktape org:list` matches the command and has no required flags in `src/config/cli/commands.ts`.
The table columns in the draft match the runtime headers in `src/commands/org-list/index.ts`: Organization, Role, Connected AWS, Current, and ID.
The role labels in the draft match the runtime labels users see for OWNER, ADMIN, DEVELOPER, and VIEWER; MEMBER is also rendered as Developer in the source.
The `<CliCommandsApiReference command="org:list" ... />` component uses the correct command name and the listed args match `logLevel`, `agent`, and `outputFormat` from the command definition.
All cross-links in the draft point to routes present in the provided navigation index.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly grounded in the command definition and implementation. The main factual gaps are an omitted empty-result behavior and an overstated related-command description for `org:delete`.

issues.statement: “The command prints a table with the following columns…”
issues.reason: The implementation prints that table only when at least one organization is returned. If no organizations are found, the command prints a warning and returns without rendering the table.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the sentence to “When organizations are found, the command prints a table…” and add a short note that an empty result prints “No organizations found for this user.”

issues.statement: Related commands: “org:delete — delete an organization you own”
issues.reason: The source describes `org:delete` as deleting organization access for the current user, with owner-only and no-other-users/no-active-connected-accounts constraints. The draft’s wording overstates the command as simply deleting an owned organization.
issues.suggestedFix: Change the related-command text to “delete organization access when owner-only constraints allow it” or a similarly narrow description.

evidence.quote:
'org:delete': {
    description: `Deletes organization access for your user.

In interactive mode, you'll pick an organization. In agent mode, provide --organizationId.

This operation is allowed only for organization OWNER and only when there are no other non-service users and no active connected AWS accounts.`,

positiveFindings: The documented command name, basic usage, and lack of required flags match `commandDefinitions['org:list']`.
The listed table headers match the implementation exactly: Organization, Role, Connected AWS, Current, and ID.
The role labels in the draft align with the implementation’s displayed labels for OWNER, ADMIN, DEVELOPER, VIEWER, and MEMBER mapped to Developer.
The `<CliCommandsApiReference command="org:list" ... />` component targets the correct command and includes the expected optional args: `logLevel`, `agent`, and `outputFormat`.

verifierResults.summary: The page covers the allowed `org:list` arguments and uses the required CLI API reference component. I found no API-completeness, union-variant, or code-example demonstrativeness blockers for this CLI reference page.
positiveFindings: All command arguments from the source are represented: `logLevel`, `agent`, and `outputFormat`.
The page correctly shows `stacktape org:list` with no required flags, matching `requiredArgs: []`.
The flags table uses `<CliCommandsApiReference command="org:list" ... />` rather than a hand-authored flags table.
Shell examples follow the one-command-per-block rule.
The documented output columns match the implementation table header in `src/commands/org-list/index.ts`.
